sections.title: A Methods
sections.content: A.1 Subjects and electrophysiological recordings All analyses were performed on publicly available in vivo extracellular electrophysiological recordings from the avian hippocampal formation (dorsomedial pallium) (H. Payne, G. Lynch, and Aronov 2021; H. L. Payne, G. F. Lynch, and Aronov 2021). The dataset comprised 755 single units recorded from food-caching black-capped chickadees (Poecile atricapillus; 39 sessions across 9 birds) and 238 single units from non-caching zebra finches (Taeniopygia guttata; 8 sessions across 9 birds), for total of 993 units. Data were loaded from MATLAB v5 binary files (RESULTS_T.mat, RESULTS_Z.mat) and parsed using custom recursive miMATRIX reader (for MCOS table objects embedded in the MAT v5 subsystem) or, where possible, using scipy.io.loadmat. Behavioral telemetry (position, head angle, movement speed) was extracted from the accompanying (behavior) variable stored as MATLAB struct. A.2 Cell-type classification Putative excitatory (E) and inhibitory (I) neurons were classified using Ward hierarchical agglomerative clustering on standardized waveform features. For each unit, two features were extracted: (i) spike width (peak-to-trough duration, converted to milliseconds) and (ii) peak-to-peak ratio (pp_ratio). Features were 𝑧-scored prior to clustering: fstd = 𝜎f Ward linkage was applied to the standardized feature matrix and the dendrogram was cut at 𝑘 = 2 clusters. The cluster with the lower mean spike width was assigned as narrow-spiking (putative inhibitory) and the remaining cluster as broad-spiking (putative excitatory). This procedure yielded classification concordance of 99% with the published labels (536/219 versus reported 538/217 for chickadees). Spatial selectivity was assessed by computing the spatial information (info) of each unit and comparing it against null distribution of 200 bin-shuffled surrogates; units with information exceeding the 99th percentile of the shuffle distribution (𝑝 < 0.01) were classified as spatially selective. A.3 Construction of population rate maps For each recording session, two-dimensional arena occupancy was discretized into 40 40 grid (𝑆 = 1,600 spatial bins). Firing rate maps were computed for each neuron as the spike count in each bin divided by the occupancy time, yielding one-dimensional vector r𝑘 R𝑆 per neuron 𝑘. Bins with no occupancy were retained as NaN rather than zero-filled; all pairwise distance computations used pairwise deletion (see below). For place field detection, which requires spatially continuous maps, NaN bins were locally replaced with zero prior to Gaussian smoothing. The population response matrix R𝑁 𝑆 was constructed by stacking the rate maps of all 𝑁 neurons recorded in given session, where each row represents neuron and each column represents spatial bin. Sessions were included in all subsequent analyses only if they contained at least 𝑁min 5 excitatory neurons; for the E/I analyses (Tier 2), both cell types required minimum of 𝑁min = 3 neurons per type. Rate maps were 𝑧-scored neuron-wise prior to all geometric analyses: 𝑟𝑘,𝑠 = 𝑟𝑘,𝑠 𝑟𝑘 𝜎𝑟𝑘 , 𝜎𝑟𝑘 > 0 Neurons with zero variance (𝜎𝑟𝑘 = 0) were assigned unit standard deviation to avoid division by zero. The per-neuron mean 𝑟𝑘 and standard deviation 𝜎𝑟𝑘 were computed using NaN-aware estimators, excluding unvisited bins from the normalization. An active-bin filter was applied, retaining only spatial bins for which at least max(2, 𝑁 /3) neurons had non-zero firing rates; sessions with fewer than 30 active bins were excluded. A.4 The Shesha metric: geometric stability of the neural manifold We quantified the geometric stability of the hippocampal spatial code using the Shesha metric (P. C. Raju 2026c), splithalf representational dissimilarity matrix (RDM) correlation framework (Kriegeskorte, Mur, and Bandettini 2008), with pairwise deletion of unvisited spatial bins. 15 Feature-split stability (SheshaFS). The population of 𝑁 neurons was randomly partitioned into two equal halves (𝑁 /2 neurons each). For each half, an RDM was computed over all active spatial bins using masked cosine distance with pairwise deletion. Let 𝑉 (ℎ) denote the set of neurons in half ℎ with valid (finite) 𝑧-scored rates at both bins 𝑖 and 𝑗. The masked cosine distance is: 𝑖 𝑗 𝑥 (ℎ) 𝑖𝑘 𝑥 (ℎ) 𝑗𝑘 𝐷 (ℎ) 𝑖 𝑗 = 1 𝑘 𝑉 (ℎ) 𝑖 𝑗 (cid:118)(cid:116) 𝑘 𝑉 (ℎ) 𝑖 𝑗 (cid:0)𝑥 (ℎ) 𝑖𝑘 (cid:1)2 (cid:118)(cid:116) 𝑘 𝑉 (ℎ) 𝑖 𝑗 (cid:0)𝑥 (ℎ) 𝑗𝑘 (cid:1)2 is the 𝑧-scored response of neuron 𝑘 (from half ℎ) at spatial bin 𝑖. Pairs with 𝑉 (ℎ) where 𝑥 (ℎ) < 2 were assigned NaN and 𝑖𝑘 excluded from downstream correlations. This pairwise deletion prevents unvisited bins (retained as NaN) from inflating similarity through shared zeros. The Shesha score is the Spearman correlation between the valid upper-triangular entries of the two RDMs: Shesha = 𝜌Spearman(cid:0)vec(D(1) valid), vec(D(2) computed only over index pairs (𝑖, 𝑗) where both 𝐷 (1) 𝑖 𝑗 and 𝐷 (2) 𝑖 𝑗 are finite. This was repeated for 𝑛splits = 100 random neuron partitions with fixed random seed (seed = 320), and the mean correlation was taken as the final Shesha score. All active bins were used (no subsampling). The Tier 2 E/I pipeline passes these parameters explicitly to feature_split; the Tier 1 pipeline calls feature_split with the shesha-geometry package defaults, which are 𝑛splits = 100, metric = cosine, and seed = 320, matching the values documented above. valid)(cid:1) 𝑖 𝑗 Sample-split stability (SheshaSS). An analogous procedure was applied using sample splits rather than feature splits: subsets comprising 40% of spatial bins were randomly drawn and RDMs computed from each subsample, yielding measure of representational robustness across spatial subsamples (sample_split(X, n_splits=30, subsample_fraction=0.4, metric=cosine, seed=320)). A.5 Mantel test for spatial topology To assess whether population-level geometric structure faithfully preserves the physical layout of the environment, we computed the Mantel test (Mantel 1967; Mantel and Valand 1970). Physical distances were computed as Euclidean distances between the 2D grid coordinates (𝑟𝑖, 𝑐𝑖 ) of each active bin: 𝑑phys (𝑖, 𝑗) = (𝑟𝑖 𝑟 𝑗 )2 + (𝑐𝑖 𝑐 𝑗 )2 Neural distances were computed as the root-mean-square distance over mutually valid neurons (pairwise deletion): 𝑑neural (𝑖, 𝑗) = (cid:118)(cid:116) 1 𝑉𝑖 𝑗 𝑘 𝑉𝑖 𝑗 ( 𝑚𝑘,𝑖 𝑚𝑘,𝑗 )2 where 𝑉𝑖 𝑗 = {𝑘 : 𝑚𝑘,𝑖 and 𝑚𝑘,𝑗 are both finite} and 𝑚𝑘,𝑖 is the 𝑧-scored firing rate of neuron 𝑘 at bin 𝑖. Pairs with 𝑉𝑖 𝑗 < 2 were excluded. When all neurons have valid data at both bins, this reduces to m𝑖 m𝑗 2/ 𝑁 . The Tier 1 Mantel analysis uses the masked formulation above (pairwise deletion over 𝑉𝑖 𝑗 ). In the Tier 2 E/I pipeline, rate maps are zero-filled prior to distance computation (nan_to_num replaces all NaN entries with zero before calling pdist), so 𝑉𝑖 𝑗 = {1, . . . , 𝑁 } for all bin 𝑁 without pairwise deletion. This zero-filling pairs and the expression reduces to standard Euclidean distance divided by is appropriate in Tier 2 because the active-bin filter has already removed unvisited bins, and the remaining bins have valid rates for all neurons within each cell-type subpopulation. The observed Mantel statistic was the Spearman rank correlation 𝑟obs between all valid (cid:0)𝑛 (cid:1) pairwise distances. Statistical significance was assessed against null distribution of 1,000 2 permutations in which bin labels were randomly shuffled, preserving marginal distributions. Only bins with at least 100 valid distance pairs were analyzed; the reported 𝑝-value is 𝑝 = (number of permutations with 𝑟perm 𝑟obs)/𝑛perm. A.6 Valiants Stable Memory Allocator theory and empirical operationalization Theoretical framework. central theoretical motivation for our analysis of hippocampal spatial codes derives from Valiants proposal that principal function of the mammalian hippocampus is to serve as stable memory allocator (SMA) for cortex (Valiant 2012). In this framework, cortex is the main locus of information storage, but hippocampus is required to identify the specific set of cortical neurons at which each new chunka conjunctive combination of previously stored itemswill be represented. Crucially, the number of neurons allocated to each new chunk must be controlled within narrow range; otherwise, successive levels of hierarchical memory allocation will cause the allocated populations to either vanish to zero or fill the cortex, rendering the system computationally unstable. Valiant formalizes this requirement through three properties that biologically plausible SMA circuit must satisfy simultaneously: 1. Stability. For wide range of input activity levels (densities) spanning more than an order of magnitudefor example, from Dense(u) [0.002, 0.025]the output density Dense(𝑓 (u)) must fall within narrow range (e.g., within 1% of target value 𝑝). Formally, circuit has 𝜖-stability in the range [𝑞, 𝑠] if there exists fixed 𝑝 such that for any input with density in [𝑞, 𝑠], Dense(𝑓 (u)) [𝑝 𝜖, 𝑝 + 𝜖]. 2. Continuity. If two input patterns and are similar (Hamming distance Ham(u, v) small), their outputs 𝑓 (u) and 𝑓 (v) should also be similar: Ham(𝑓 (u), 𝑓 (v)) 𝛾 Ham(u, v). This ensures noise tolerancesmall perturbations in neural activity do not catastrophically alter the allocation. 3. Orthogonality. If two inputs differ substantially, their outputs must also differ sufficiently to be treated by cortex as distinct items: Ham(𝑓 (u), 𝑓 (v)) 𝛿 Ham(u, v). This prevents catastrophic interference between memories. Circuit construction. Valiant demonstrates that all three properties can be realized by shallow feedforward network of two or three layers with random connectivity. The key construction uses threshold function 𝑥 +𝑦 + 𝑧 2𝑡 1 on four randomly chosen inputs, where 𝑥, 𝑦, 𝑧 are excitatory and 𝑡 is inhibitory. If fraction 𝑝 of inputs are active, the probability that any output neuron fires is: ℎ(𝑝) = (1 𝑝) (cid:0)1 (1 𝑝)3(cid:1) + 𝑝4 = 4𝑝3 6𝑝2 + 3𝑝 This function has fixed point at 𝑝 = 1/2 with derivative ℎ (𝑝) = 0, guaranteeing super-exponential convergence. For biologically realistic low-density regimes (𝑝 0.01), the construction is extended to 𝑥 + 𝑦 + 𝑧 2𝑡 1 where 𝑡 = 1[(𝑡1 + + 𝑡𝑘 ) 1] with 𝑘 (ln 3)/𝑝 inhibitory inputs, yielding fixed point near the target density with derivative ℎ (𝑝) 1 ln 3 0.099. The construction achieves 3-continuity (output disagreement at most 3 times input disagreement per layer) and (0.719 . . .)𝑖 -orthogonality after 𝑖 layers throughout the input range, with expansion approaching 3/2 for small perturbations. Biological correspondences. The SMA architecture maps onto the known anatomy of the hippocampal system: the entorhinal cortex serves as the down/up transformer (compressing 1010 cortical neurons to 107 hippocampal neurons and back), while the intrinsic hippocampal circuitry (dentate gyrus CA3 CA1) realizes the inner SMA. The construction requires effectively random connectivity between layersconsistent with the observed decorrelation of place fields among anatomically neighboring hippocampal neurons (Redish et al. 2001; Thompson and Best 1990)and operates at sparse activity levels (𝑝 0.01), consistent with recordings from medial temporal lobe showing densities of 0.005 0.012 (Waydo et al. 2006). Neurogenesis in the dentate gyrus can be interpreted within this framework as mechanism for time-stamping allocations: the steady replacement of input neurons changes the SMA function at controlled rate, bounded by the continuity property, such that the same stimulus encountered months apart will be allocated to sufficiently different cortical set to support distinct episodic memories. Predictions for spatial codes. The SMA theory generates three specific, testable predictions for hippocampal spatial representations that we operationalize in this study. First, the number of neurons allocated to represent each spatial location should be controlled within narrow range (stability), predicting low CV in population overlap counts across spatial bins. Second, the territory assigned to each neuron should be consistent in size (a corollary of stability applied to the neuron level), predicting low CV in place field areas. Third, the allocation pattern should be reproducible across independent subsamples of the population (continuity), predicting high split-half reliability of per-bin neuron counts. In food-caching species that must reliably encode thousands of cache locations, these SMA properties should be under stronger selective pressureand hence more pronouncedthan in non-caching species. Empirical metrics. Three metrics were designed to isolate allocation stability from tuning quality, each directly linked to specific SMA prediction: 17 For each neuron, the 2D rate map was smoothed with Gaussian kernel (𝜎 = 1.0 bins). Place Place field detection. fields were defined as contiguous regions where the smoothed rate exceeded 30% of the neurons peak rate. Connected components were identified via scipy.ndimage.label; fragments smaller than 4 bins were discarded as noise. Neurons with peak rates below 0.5 Hz were classified as non-spatial. Metric 1: Field-size consistency. The CV of place field areas (in bins) across all neurons with detected fields was computed. low CV indicates that each neuron is allocated territory of consistent size, as predicted by Valiant-like memory allocation model. For each spatial bin falling within at least one neurons place field, we Metric 2: Population overlap consistency. counted the number of co-active neurons (i.e., neurons whose field mask covered that bin). The CV of this count across field bins quantifies the consistency of ensemble recruitment; low CV indicates that each location activates similarly sized neural ensemble. Metric 3: Split-half allocation reliability. The neuron population was randomly partitioned 50 times. For each split, we counted the number of field-active neurons per bin in each half and computed the Spearman correlation between the two halves. The mean correlation across splits measures the reproducibility of the spatial allocation pattern. The onetailed MannWhitney test for this metric used alternative=less (chickadee < finch), because the double-dissociation predicts that caching birds exhibit lower allocation reliabilitytheir caching neurons actively shift ensemble membership across trials, consistent with geometric attractor mechanism rather than fixed ensemble assignment. A.7 Alternative geometric stability benchmarks To address concerns about whether the Shesha metric, as novel measure, might yield results not reproducible by established alternatives, we implemented two additional stability metrics and verified that all three agree directionally on the same sessions. Test-retest population vector (PV) correlation. The neuron population was randomly partitioned into two halves (𝑛splits = 100, seed = 320). For each split, the population vectorthe mean firing rate per spatial bin, averaged over all neurons in that halfwas computed for each half. The Spearman rank correlation between the two PV profiles was computed over bins where at least one half had non-zero activity ( 30 active bins required). The mean across splits is the PV stability score. This metric is the simplest interpretable alternative: it measures whether the aggregate spatial preference of random neuron subset is reproducible, without making any geometric assumptions. Canonical correlation analysis (CCA) stability. Neuron halves were used directly as feature matrices. For each split, let X1 R𝑛bins 𝑘1 and X2 R𝑛bins 𝑘2 denote the 𝑧-scored activity matrices of the two halves over active bins, where 𝑘1, 𝑘2 are the respective half-sizes. Regularized within-group covariance matrices were formed: C11 = 1 X1 𝑛bins + 106 I, C22 = 2 X2 𝑛bins + 10 I, C12 = 1 X2 𝑛bins The cross-covariance was whitened using Cholesky factors of the inverse within-group covariances: = C1/2 11 C12 C1/2 The singular values 𝜎1 𝜎2 of T, clipped to [0, 1], are the canonical correlations. The CCA stability score is the mean of the top 𝑑 = min(3, 𝑘1 1, 𝑘2 1) canonical correlations, averaged over 𝑛splits = 50 random neuron partitions. The number of components was chosen adaptivelyrather than requiring fixed minimum half-size, 𝑑 was reduced whenever halves were smallto avoid discarding all splits for low-neuron sessions (which would yield spurious NaN rather than meaningful low score). Cholesky decomposition failure (near-singular covariance, caught as LinAlgError) discarded only that split. 18 For each random neuron split, the pairwise cosine distance matrix between Procrustes-aligned split-half error. spatial bins (not neurons) was computed from each half. Let D(ℎ) R𝑛bins 𝑛bins be the squareform cosine distance matrix from half ℎ, where distances are taken between rows of (i.e., between bin-level activity patterns projected into ℎ the half-neuron space). An orthogonal Procrustes alignment was applied to D(2) relative to D(1) , finding the rotation = arg minRR=I D(1) D(2) R𝐹 . The normalized Frobenius residual after alignment is: 𝜀 = D(1) D(2) 𝐹 D(1) 𝐹 Procrustes stability is reported as 1 𝜀 (mean residual over 𝑛splits = 100 splits), so that higher values indicate better geometric reproducibility. This metric aligns the full pairwise distance structure directlywithout dimensionality reduction or embeddingand is therefore embedding-free. For each of the three metrics (Shesha, PV correlation, CCA stability), one-sided MannWhitney Concordance check. 𝑈 test (chickadee > finch) was computed over the session-level scores. Directional consistency (chickadee mean > finch mean) was assessed for each metric independently and recorded in concordance summary table. All three metrics agreeing directionally constitutes the primary concordance result. Procrustes-aligned split-half stability was additionally computed as supplementary benchmark (see above) but was not included in the primary concordance set. A.8 Within-session representational drift Temporal stability of individual neuron rate maps was assessed using the cross-correlation of first-half versus second-half maps (xcorr_map), as provided in the original dataset. Species differences were tested using one-sided MannWhitney 𝑈 tests (chickadee > finch). A.9 Anteriorposterior gradient For chickadee sessions with histologically confirmed recording-site subdivisions (available in the subdivision column of the dataset), Shesha scores were computed per subdivision and compared using MannWhitney 𝑈 tests to assess whether geometric stability varies along the anteriorposterior axis of the hippocampal formation. A.10 Neuron-count-matched downsampling control To control for the possibility that species differences in Shesha reflect differences in recorded neuron counts rather than genuine geometric stability, we performed neuron-count-matched downsampling analysis. The target neuron count 𝑁target was set to the median number of neurons per session in the zebra finch dataset. For each chickadee session with at least 𝑁target neurons, random subset of 𝑁target neurons was drawn without replacement, Shesha was recomputed on the subsampled population, and this procedure was repeated 50 times per session. The per-session mean across subsamples was taken as the neuron-matched Shesha score. The distribution of matched chickadee scores was compared to the original finch distribution using one-sided MannWhitney 𝑈 tests (chickadee > finch) and bootstrapped Cohens 𝑑 with 95% confidence intervals (𝑛boot = 10,000). Results are reported alongside the unmatched analysis in the negative controls output. A.11 Tier 1 negative controls for geometric stability To rule out the possibility that the elevated Shesha scores in chickadees reflect single-neuron tuning properties rather than genuine population-level geometric structure, two additional negative controls were applied to every session passing the minimum neuron criterion (𝑁 5): Circular shift control. Each neurons one-dimensional rate map was circularly shifted by uniformly random offset drawn independently for each neuron. This preserves the marginal firing-rate distribution and spatial smoothness of individual neurons but destroys the inter-neuron spatial relationships that define population geometry. For each session, 20 shifted populations were generated and Shesha was computed on each; the per-session control score was the mean across the 20 iterations. 19 Map shuffle control. Each neurons rate-map bins were randomly permuted (without replacement), independently across neurons. This eliminates spatial continuity within each neurons map and therefore destroys manifold smoothness at both the single-neuron and population levels. As above, 20 shuffled populations were generated per session and the per-session control score was the mean across iterations. If the species difference in Shesha reflects population geometry, original scores should substantially exceed both controls. collapse of the species difference under either control would indicate that single-neuron statistics, rather than coordinated population structure, drive the observed effect. A.12 Excitatory-inhibitory circuit analysis For each chickadee session meeting the minimum neuron criterion ( 3 neurons and 3 E/I decomposition. neurons), population matrices were constructed separately for excitatory (M𝐸), inhibitory (M𝐼 ), and combined (Mall) subpopulations. Shesha was computed independently for each. Residual contribution analysis. To isolate the unique geometric information carried by each cell type, we subtracted the mean spatial map of the opposite type from each neurons rate map prior to computing Shesha. The shared variance between and populations was quantified as the Spearman correlation between their mean spatial maps over active bins. Partial information decomposition (PID). simplified PID was computed using Shesha as proxy for geometric information content. Redundant information was defined as min(Shesha𝐸, Shesha𝐼 ); unique and contributions as the respective excesses over the redundant component; and synergistic information as max(0, Sheshaall (Shesha𝐸 +Shesha𝐼 redundant)), capturing geometric structure that emerges only from the combined population. E-I coordination index. The Spearman rank correlation between per-session Shesha𝐸 and Shesha𝐼 values was computed to assess whether and geometric stability co-vary across recording sessions. Spatial frequency analysis. Mean population rate maps were computed for and populations in each session. 2D fast Fourier transform was applied and the power spectrum was radially averaged. The Spearman correlation between the normalized and power spectra quantified spectral similarity. Dominant spatial frequencies were identified as the non-DC peak of the radial power spectrum. Dimensionality analysis. The intrinsic dimensionality of each subpopulation was estimated via PCA on the transposed (bins neurons) rate map matrix, recording the number of principal components required to explain 95% of the variance. Subspace overlap between and representations was quantified as the absolute Pearson correlation between the first principal components of each subpopulation. Temporal dynamics. To distinguish between the geometric stability captured by the feature-split Shesha metric (SheshaFS) and complementary temporal stability reflecting trial-to-trial consistency, we computed sample-split variant of Shesha (SheshaSS) for each and subpopulation. Sample-split stability was computed by randomly subsampling 40% of the spatial bins across 30 bootstrap iterations (subsample_fraction = 0.4, 𝑛splits = 30, cosine metric, seed 320) and correlating the pair of resulting RDMs, yielding measure of how stable the representational geometry is across different subsets of the spatial context rather than across different subsets of neurons. For each session, SheshaFS (feature-split, neurons partitioned) and SheshaSS (sample-split, bins subsampled) were computed separately for the and populations, and the ratio SheshaFS / SheshaSS was reported as scale-free index of how much the geometric structure depends on the spatial coverage relative to the neuron identity. Per-session values for both stability types and the ratio are exported to supplementary data. This analysis is restricted to chickadee sessions (the finch inhibitory population is insufficient for separate E/I comparisons) and uses the global minimum neuron threshold (𝑛𝐸, 𝑛𝐼 3). E/I principal subspace angle analysis. To provide better-powered and more direct test of the claim that and populations occupy distinct representational subspacescomplementing the underpowered session-level E/I Shesha correlationwe computed the principal angles between the and principal subspaces. For each session, the top-𝑑 left 𝐼 were extracted via SVD (where rows are spatial bins and columns are neurons), yielding singular vectors of 𝐸 and 20 orthonormal bases Q𝐸, Q𝐼 R𝑛bins 𝑑 representing the dominant directions of spatial activity variation in each population. The principal angles 𝜃1 𝜃2 𝜃𝑑 between the two subspaces were computed from the singular values 𝜎1 𝜎𝑑 of 𝐸 Q𝐼 : 𝜃𝑖 = arccos(𝜎𝑖 ), 𝜎𝑖 = clip(𝜎𝑖, 1, 1) with 𝑑 = min(3, 𝑛𝐸 1, 𝑛𝐼 1, 𝑛bins1). Sessions with min(𝑛𝐸, 𝑛bins) < 𝑑 were excluded. Mean principal angle across the top𝑑 dimensions was computed per session; angles approaching 90 indicate orthogonal (non-overlapping) subspaces, while small angles indicate shared representational structure. This analysis does not depend on single-session correlations and is substantially better powered than the session-level E/I Shesha coordination correlation (𝑟 = 0.333, 𝑝 = 0.381), providing an independent test of whether the claim of orthogonal E/I dynamics is supported by the data. The minimum neuron requirement for this analysis is 𝑛𝐸 3 and 𝑛𝐼 3 (matching the global session-inclusion criterion MIN_NEURONS_TYPE = 3), because SVD is well-defined for any matrix with at least as many columns as the target subspace dimension. This yields 𝑛 = 21 sessions and is deliberately the least restrictive threshold used in Tier 2. As sensitivity check, the analysis was repeated with strict threshold of 𝑛𝐸 4 and 𝑛𝐼 4 (matching the Shesha split-half requirement), reducing the sample to the same 𝑛 = 12 sessions used by the paired synergy test; principal angle estimates from both thresholds are reported in the supplementary data and should agree if the result is robust to the three borderline sessions. Paired full-population vs. E-only synergy test. The claim that the combined E/I population exhibits higher geometric stability than the excitatory population alonei.e. that inhibitory neurons contribute synergistically to manifold structure rather than merely adding noiserequires direct paired within-session test. This analysis operates on subset of the sessions used for subspace angles (𝑛 = 12 vs. 𝑛 = 21) for the following reason: the Shesha split-half procedure requires at least 4 neurons per subpopulation to form two non-trivial halves of 2 neurons each, so sessions with exactly 𝑛𝐸 = 3 pass the global inclusion criterion (MIN_NEURONS_TYPE = 3) and therefore contribute to the subspace angle analysis, but return NaN from the split-half computation and are consequently excluded from the paired test. The nine-session difference reflects this deliberate asymmetry: SVD-based subspace estimation is valid at 𝑛𝐸 = 3 (one vector per subspace dimension), while split-half RDM correlation is degenerate with only one neuron per half. All sessions satisfying each analysiss respective minimum neuron criterion were included; no sessions were selectively excluded. As cross-check on both analyses simultaneously, we ran Shesha with relaxed threshold (𝑛𝐸 3, allowing sessions with degenerate one-neuron half) and the subspace angle analysis with strict threshold (𝑛𝐸 4). This produces two additional sample-matched comparisons: (i) relaxed Shesha paired synergy test at 𝑛 = 21 sessions, matching the primary subspace angle sample; and (ii) strict subspace angle analysis at 𝑛 = 12 sessions, matching the primary paired synergy test. Concordance across all four variantsprimary strict Shesha, relaxed Shesha, primary relaxed subspace angles, strict subspace anglesconstitutes the robustness check for the threshold-sensitivity concern. For each session in which both Sheshafull and Shesha𝐸 were computable (non-NaN), we formed the signed difference Δ𝑠 = Sheshafull,𝑠 Shesha𝐸,𝑠 . The number of sessions with Δ𝑠 > 0 was recorded, and the one-sided Wilcoxon signedrank test (𝐻1: Sheshafull > Shesha𝐸) was applied to the paired observations. The Wilcoxon test was chosen over paired 𝑡-test because Shesha scores are bounded in [1, 1] and their differences cannot be assumed normally distributed at the small session counts available. This test is strictly more informative than the unpaired vs. comparison (𝑝 = 0.939), which addresses different contrast; the synergy claim requires the paired Full vs. E-only test. Per-session values of Sheshafull, Shesha𝐸, Shesha𝐼 , and Δ𝑠 are reported in the supplementary data alongside the test statistic 𝑊 and one-sided 𝑝-value. Negative controls. 1. Anti-correlated population: An artificial population was generated by sign-inverting the population and rescaling to non-negative values, establishing an upper bound on E/I conflict. 2. Random pairing: Each sessions neurons were paired with neurons drawn from different session, breaking within-session E-I coordination while preserving marginal statistics. 3. Scaled noise injection: Graded Gaussian noise (𝜎 {0.0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0}) was added to the populations rate maps to identify the noise level at which geometric stability degrades. Species comparisons employed MannWhitney 𝑈 tests (two-sided or one-sided as specified). The Statistical inference. paired Full vs. E-only synergy test used the one-sided Wilcoxon signed-rank test on session-level differences (𝐻1: Δ > 0). 21 Within-chickadee E/I differences were further assessed using: Simple bootstrap confidence intervals (𝑛boot = 10,000; 95% percentile CIs), appropriate for within-species comparisons. Cohens 𝑑 effect sizes with bootstrap CIs (𝑛boot = 10,000). Non-parametric permutation tests (𝑛perm = 10,000). Jackknife resampling to verify that results were not driven by single influential sessions. MetropolisHastings Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC; 𝑛MCMC = 1,000 samples, 50% burn-in, yielding 500 postburn-in samples) for Bayesian credible intervals on the E-I difference, assuming Gaussian likelihood with uniform priors. A.13 Computational model of topological capacity Synthetic neural populations were generated as follows. 𝑁 = 500 neurons were assigned Population code generation. uniformly random preferred locations on 1D circular track [0, 1) (the supplementary phase-transition boundary and extended temporal-drift simulations used 𝑁 = 400 for computational tractability; all other parameters were identical). Each neurons tuning curve was modeled as Gaussian bump: 𝑟 𝑗 (𝑥) = exp(cid:16) 𝑗 )2 𝑑 (𝑥, 𝑥 2𝜎 2 (cid:17) , 𝑑 (𝑥, 𝑦) = min(𝑥 𝑦, 1 𝑥 𝑦) where 𝑥 𝑗 is the preferred location and 𝜎 = 𝜌/2 with sparsity 𝜌 = 0.15 (standard configuration). Functional noise was added as i.i.d. Gaussian perturbations (𝜎noise = 0.2), and negative rates were rectified. The topology strength parameter 𝜏 [0, 1] controlled the fraction of neurons retaining their topological tuning curves; the remaining (1 𝜏)𝑁 neurons had their tuning curves randomly permuted across locations. Population vectors were L2-normalized. Three canonical regimes were defined: Regime Topology 𝜏 Biological analogue Crystal Mist Noise Chickadee (food-cacher) Zebra finch (non-cacher) Null (no topology) 1.0 0.5 0.0 The achieved topology fidelity of each regime was verified by computing the Mantel 𝑟 (Spearman correlation between pairwise physical and neural Euclidean distances) at 𝑀 = 100 locations, averaged over 15 independent random seeds (320, 1991, 9, 7258, 7, 2222, 724, 3, 12, 108, 18, 11, 1754, 411, 103) to obtain stable estimates. Decoders. Four decoding strategies were compared: 1. Noisy nearest-neighbor (NN): Independent Gaussian readout noise (𝜎read = 0.3) was added to each element of the population vector. The noisy vector was then matched against the noiseless template library by Euclidean distance (self-match excluded via 𝐷𝑖𝑖 = ). Decoding performance was quantified as the misclassification ratethe fraction of locations for which the nearest-neighbor template did not correspond to the true locationrather than continuous circular distance. This discrete error metric is unambiguous when the stimulus set is finite set of stored locations and avoids the implicit assumption of smoothly varying error surface. 2. Linear (Ridge regression): RidgeCV decoder (𝛼 {0.1, 1.0, 10.0}) was trained on noiseless population vectors and evaluated on noisy inputs. 3. Support vector regression (SVR): An 𝜖-SVR with RBF kernel (𝐶 = 1.0, 𝜖 = 0.1) was trained on noiseless codes and tested on noisy readout. 4. Ideal Bayesian: Maximum posteriori estimation under Gaussian noise model with uniform prior, ˆ𝑥 = arg max𝑥 𝑃 (r𝑥) = arg min𝑥 (cid:205)𝑗 (𝑟 𝑗 𝜇 𝑗 (𝑥))2/(2𝜎 2 read). For the NN decoder, error was the misclassification rate 𝜖NN = 1 1 𝑖=1 1[ ˆ𝑥𝑖 = 𝑥𝑖 ], where ˆ𝑥𝑖 is the nearest-neighbor 𝑀 template and 𝑥𝑖 is the true location. For the regression-based decoders (Ridge, SVR, Bayesian), error was the mean circular distance 𝜖 = min(𝑥 ˆ𝑥 , 1 𝑥 ˆ𝑥 ) averaged over test locations. (cid:205)𝑀 22 Memory capacity. Capacity was tested by increasing the number of stored locations 𝑀 {10, 20, 30, . . . , 1000} and measuring the NN misclassification rate at each memory load. The critical capacity was defined as the memory load at which the misclassification rate exceeded threshold set at the midpoint between crystal and noise error rates at 𝑀 = 100 locations; interpolation between adjacent loads refined the estimate. Negative controls. 1. Matched-random control: Random codes were generated by searching over topology strengths to match the Mantel 𝑟 of the target code (Δ𝑟 < 0.05), controlling for total distance-preserving structure. 2. Anti-topological control: The distancesimilarity relationship was inverted (𝑑inv = 0.5 𝑑), producing codes where spatially distant locations have similar representations. 3. Dimension-matched control: Random Gaussian codes were projected onto subspace of specified dimensionality via SVD truncation, then sparsified and normalized, controlling for effective rank. Noise robustness. The topology advantage was stress-tested under three noise models: Additive Gaussian noise: Readout noise scaled from 𝜎 = 0.1 to 1.0 (10 levels). Multiplicative (Poisson-like) noise: Signal-proportional noise, 𝑟 = 𝑟 (1 + 𝛼 𝑧), 𝑧 (0, 1), with gain factor 𝛼 [0.1, 2.0] (10 levels). Correlated noise: fraction 𝑐 {0.0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8} of the noise variance was shared across neurons: 𝜼 = 1 𝑐 𝜼indep. 𝑐 𝜂common+ The critical noise thresholdthe readout noise level at which the crystalmist performance difference fell below threshold of 0.1was identified by sweeping 20 noise levels from 0.1 to 2.0. Information-theoretic bounds. theoretical lower bound on capacity was computed using Shannon channel capacity under Gaussian channel model: 𝐶lower = 1 2 (cid:32) log2 1 + (cid:33) 𝜌𝑁 𝜎 2 read bits where 𝜌𝑁 approximates the sparse signal power. An upper bound was estimated as 𝐶upper = log2(𝑁 /𝜌), based on the maximum number of distinguishable patterns. Empirical information content was estimated from nearest-neighbor distances in the neural space. Topology sweep. parametric sweep over topology strength (𝜏 {0.0, 0.1, 0.2, . . . , 1.0}, 11 levels) was conducted at fixed memory load of 100 locations with 250 independent trials per level. Means and 95% percentile confidence intervals were computed for both decoding error and Mantel 𝑟 . 2D open-field arena validation. The primary capacity simulation used 1D circular track to keep the geometry tractable across large parameter sweeps. To verify that the key claims generalize to the open-field arena geometry used in the empirical recordings, we ran targeted 2D simulation with 210 network configurations (10 trials 7 memory loads 3 regimes). Tuning curves were 2D isotropic Gaussian place fields: 𝑟 𝑗 (x) = exp (cid:18) c𝑗 2 2𝜎 2 (cid:19) , x, c𝑗 [0, 1]2 where c𝑗 Uniform([0, 1]2) is the preferred location of neuron 𝑗 and 𝜎 = 𝜌/2 with 𝜌 = 0.15 (matching the 1D configuration and the normalized empirical 4040 arena). Physical distances were 2D Euclidean rather than circular. The noisy nearest-neighbor error was the misclassification rate (fraction of 2D locations decoded to the wrong template), consistent with the updated error metric used throughout Tier 3. All other simulation parameters (𝑁 = 500, 𝜎noise = 0.2, 𝜎read = 0.3) were held identical to the 1D case to isolate the effect of arena geometry. The Mantel statistic used 2D Euclidean physical distances. We report: (i) the directional ordering of misclassification rates (crystal mist noise) across all seven memory loads; (ii) the critical capacity for each regime; and (iii) phase diagram of error as function of both topology strength 23 and memory load, visualizing where the phase transition occurs in 2D parameter space. All results and per-load error rates are exported to supplementary data. Empirical redundancy. Redundancy was quantified from the real Payne data as 𝑅 = (cid:205)𝑘: 𝐼𝑘 >0 𝐼𝑘 𝐼pop where 𝐼𝑘 is the spatial information of individual neuron 𝑘 and 𝐼pop is the population-level spatial information estimated via cross-validated Ridge regression (𝛼 {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0}; 5-fold CV). The decoded variables were the row and column indices of each spatial bin, and population information was computed as 𝐼pop = 1 2 log2(1𝑅2) where 𝑅2 is the mean crossvalidated coefficient of determination across both spatial dimensions. Species differences in redundancy were tested using two-sided MannWhitney 𝑈 tests. Because the redundancy ratio diverges as 𝐼pop 0, strict floor of 𝐼pop 0.05 bits was applied: sessions below this threshold were excluded from filtered redundancy analysis, and both unfiltered and filtered results are reported to demonstrate robustness. Absolute population information with bootstrap confidence intervals. To supplement the redundancy ratio with interpretable absolute quantities, we additionally report 𝐼pop in bits per session with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals. For each session, neurons were resampled with replacement (𝑛boot = 500 iterations), 𝐼pop was recomputed on each bootstrap replicate via the same cross-validated Ridge regression procedure, and the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles of the bootstrap distribution were taken as the CI bounds. Sessions for which the point estimate 𝐼pop was NaN (insufficient active bins for cross-validation) were excluded. Per-session absolute 𝐼pop values are reported alongside the redundancy ratios, enabling readers to verify that the observed redundancy differences are not artifacts of sessions with near-zero 𝐼pop. A.14 10,000-configuration parameter sweep To assess the generality of the topology advantage across biologically plausible parameter regimes, comprehensive sweep was conducted over 20 25 20 = 10,000 combinations of: Population size 𝑁 {25, 50, 75, . . . , 500} (20 values) Sparsity 𝜌 {0.01, 0.02, 0.03, . . . , 0.25} (25 values) Number of independent trials 𝑇 {25, 50, 75, . . . , 500} (20 values) For each combination, all four decoders were evaluated at fixed memory load of 100 locations under crystal (𝜏 = 1.0), mist (𝜏 = 0.5), and noise (𝜏 = 0.0) regimes. Capacity advantage was defined as the difference in NN error between the random and topological codes. Note that the NN error metric used throughout the parameter sweep is the mean circular distance (not the misclassification rate used in the targeted capacity analysis); this sweep predates the metric update, but the qualitative conclusioncrystal < mist < noise ordering is robust across all 10,000 parameter combinationsis unchanged because both metrics rank the three regimes identically. The sweep was executed in batches of 3,500 combinations (to accommodate the Google Colab 24-hour runtime limit) with incremental checkpointing every 100 combinations. Batch results were combined post hoc into single dataset for analysis. Summary statistics were computed by marginalizing over each parameter dimension. A.15 Statistical framework All species comparisons (Tier 1) employed one-sided MannWhitney 𝑈 tests (chickadee > finch) where the theoretical prediction specified direction, and two-sided tests otherwise. An important exception is the split-half allocation reliability metric (Metric 3), for which the one-tailed test direction is chickadee < finch: the double-dissociation hypothesis predicts that caching birds show lower allocation reliability (i.e., more dynamic ensemble membership) alongside higher geometric stability. Reversing this direction would test the wrong tail and yield the complement of the correct 𝑝-value. Within-species E/I comparisons (Tier 2) used simple (non-hierarchical) bootstrap, which is statistically appropriate because no cross-species inference is required. Confidence intervals are reported at the 95% level throughout. All simulations used fixed random seeds (320) for exact reproducibility. Figures present individual data points alongside summary statistics. 24 The three alternative stability benchmarks (Shesha, PV correlation, CCA stability; Tier 1) were treated as pre-specified concordance set: each metric was tested independently, and the primary claim of species difference is supported only if all three agree directionally. This criterion mitigates the risk of over-interpreting any single novel metric; the concordance summary reporting means, 𝑝-values, and directional consistency flags for each metric is provided in the supplementary data. A.16 Software and code availability All analyses were implemented in Python 3 using NumPy, SciPy, pandas, scikit-learn, matplotlib, and tqdm. The Shesha metric is implemented in the open-source shesha-geometry package (P. C. Raju 2026e), available on the Python Package Index (https://pypi.org/project/shesha-geometry/). Custom analysis code, including all tiered analyses and the parameter sweep framework, is available at https://github.com/prashantcraju/hippocampal-stability. The primary Tier 1 analysis encompasses the Shesha metric, the revised Valiant allocation metrics, the three alternative stability benchmarks (test-retest PV correlation, CCA stability, Procrustes-aligned split-half), the negative controls (circular shift, map shuffle, and neuron-count-matched downsampling), and the concordance verification across all three benchmarks. The Tier 2 E/I analysis is implemented in two complementary pipelines: the primary pipeline (tier2_ei_stability.py) produces Shesha by cell type, the Mantel test, spatial information, within-session stability, E/I spatial correlation, principal subspace angle analysis, the paired full-population vs. E-only synergy test, and the associated negative controls; supplementary pipeline (tier2_enhanced.py) contributes the residual contribution analysis, partial information decomposition, spatial frequency analysis, dimensionality analysis, temporal dynamics (SheshaSS), and Bayesian MCMC inference. The Tier 3 analysis covers the 1D computational capacity simulation, the 10,000-configuration parameter sweep, the 2D open-field arena validation, the topology sensitivity sweep, and the empirical redundancy analysis including per-session absolute 𝐼pop with bootstrap confidence intervals.

sections.title: B Figures
sections.content: Figure S1: Cluster-sorted RDMs and topogeographic correspondence. (A, B) RDMs for the best-session chickadee (A) and finch (B), re-sorted by Ward hierarchical clustering on the RDM itself rather than by physical arena order. Physicalcluster agreement (Spearman 𝑟 between physical rank ordering and cluster leaf ordering) is 𝑟 = 0.446 for the chickadee and 𝑟 = 0.217 for the finch. (C) Binned mean SEM of neural cosine distance as function of physical distance between bin pairs (best session per species). The chickadee shows monotonically increasing physicalneural distance relationship; the finch curve remains near zero across all physical distances, indicating that neural population vectors carry negligible spatial distance information. (D) Physicalcluster rank correlation across all sessions (Mann Whitney 𝑈 , 𝑝 = 0.031). Chickadee sessions exhibit broadly distributed agreement values with median near zero and negative tail, while finch sessions show consistently positive agreement. This pattern reflects qualitative difference in representational geometry: the chickadee hippocampus organizes spatial representations into rich, multi-scale cluster hierarchy whose dominant axes are not aligned with the cardinal row-major physical ordering used here, whereas the finch cluster structure is weaker overall and more consistent with simple topographic layout. The chickadees negative physicalcluster 𝑟 values thus reflect the complexity of its geometric organization rather than an absence of spatial structure; the high Mantel 𝑟 values in Figure 1C confirm that topogeographic correspondence is in fact stronger in chickadees at the population level. 26 Figure S2: Double dissociation between geometric stability and discrete memory allocation. (A) Place field size heterogeneity. Caching networks exhibit significantly higher coefficient of variation (CV) in place field sizes, defying the Stable Memory Allocator (SMA) prediction that individual locations recruit consistent, uniform computational territories. (B) Split-half allocation reliability. The caching hippocampus exhibits near-zero reliability in recruiting the same number of active neurons across subsets, actively refuting the hypothesis of rigid, discrete cellular allocation. (C) The double dissociation. Caching networks achieve extreme geometric rigidity (high Shesha) despite functionally zero discrete allocation reliability. This demonstrates that spatial memory capacity is stored in the continuous topology of the population manifold rather than in rigid, discrete cellular assignments. (D) Anterior-Posterior anatomical gradient of geometric stability within the caching chickadee hippocampus, demonstrating the distribution of manifold rigidity across subdivisions. 27 (A) Neuron-matched downsampling Figure S3: Methodological controls and alternative stability benchmarks. control. Chickadee ensembles were randomly downsampled to match the median finch neuron count (𝑁 = 6; 30/39 sessions eligible). The geometric stability advantage persists (Cohens 𝑑 = 0.560, 𝑝 = 0.025), confirming the effect reflects genuine population structure rather than recording depth. (B) Spatial permutation controls. Circular shifting, which preserves individual spatial tuning statistics but misaligns the population vector, substantially reduces geometric stability in both species (chickadee: 0.241 0.154; finch: 0.171 0.072). Full map shuffling completely abolishes it (SheshaFS 0.000), verifying that rigidity requires authentic, coordinated spatial continuity across the full population. (C) Split-half population vector (PV) correlation. This traditional linear rate-map metric fails to detect the caching advantage (𝑝 = 0.223), demonstrating that geometric rigidity is not carried by standard firing rate reliability. (D) Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA) stability. This independent geometry-sensitive measure, quantifying shared subspace structure across neuron splits, shows trend toward caching advantage (chickadee 0.554 vs. finch 0.481, 𝑝 = 0.087), corroborating the Shesha framework and confirming that capacity is encoded in higher-order manifold geometry rather than individual firing rates. Figure S4: Excitatory-inhibitory dissociation of spatial information and temporal stability. (A) Spatial information content (bits/spike) of individual excitatory (E) and inhibitory (I) neurons. Excitatory cells carry significantly more spatial information than inhibitory cells in both species, confirming cells as the primary locus of sharp spatial tuning. (B) Within-session temporal stability (split-half correlation of spatial rate maps). Despite lacking sharp spatial tuning, inhibitory cells exhibit temporal reliability that is statistically indistinguishable from excitatory cells, consistent with robust, broad contextual or gain signal rather than mere recording noise. (C) Topogeographic structure evaluated via the Mantel test (𝑟 ). The strict physical-neural structural correspondence is predominantly driven by the excitatory subpopulation. (D) The in silico Knockout Test. The absolute geometric rigidity (Shesha) of the network drops significantly when inhibitory neurons are artificially removed from the population code (E-only), demonstrating that intact I-cell dynamics are structurally required to stabilise the high-capacity E-cell geometry. 29 Figure S5: Topology advantage is robust across 10,000 network configurations. Each panel shows distinct sparsity level (𝜌 = 0.010.25); within each panel, the 𝑥-axis is population size (𝑁 = 25500 neurons) and the 𝑦-axis is the number of trials (𝑇 = 25500). Color encodes the topology advantage ΔError = Errorrandom Errorcrystal, where higher values indicate greater benefit of topological organization over an unstructured network. At very low sparsity (𝜌 = 0.01) the advantage is near zero regardless of 𝑁 or 𝑇 , indicating that insufficient spatial coverage prevents any code from organizing stable manifold. The advantage rises steeply between 𝜌 = 0.02 and 𝜌 = 0.10 and saturates at approximately 𝜌 = 0.11 (median ΔError 0.180), beyond which further increases in sparsity yield diminishing returns. Critically, the advantage is uniform across the full range of population sizes and trial counts within each panel, demonstrating that the topology benefit is not an artifact of particular sample-size regime. The highlighted panel (green border, 𝜌 = 0.15) corresponds to the empirical sparsity of chickadee hippocampal neurons in the Payne dataset, which falls squarely within the saturated high-capacity regime (median ΔError = 0.183). These results confirm that the >100-fold capacity advantage reported in the main text is robust to variation in network scale and data quantity across the full parameter space explored. 30 Figure S6: Representative raw spatial firing rate maps. (A) Eight highly informative excitatory neurons from the food-caching chickadee. The rate maps demonstrate the highly heterogeneous, multi-field crystalline tuning structure characteristic of the caching hippocampus. Unvisited spatial bins are masked in light grey. Peak firing rate (Hz) and spatial information (bits/spike) are inset for each cell. (B) Eight highly informative excitatory neurons from the noncaching finch. Even among the most spatially selective cells in the finch population, spatial tuning is characterised by diffuse, single-field or un-tuned mist-like firing that lacks the sharp topographic boundaries required for high-capacity rigid manifold. Figure S7: Single-unit correlates of population-level geometric stability in food-caching versus non-caching In vivo hippocampal recordings from the food-caching black-capped chickadee (titmouse, red; 𝑛 = 755 units) birds. and the non-caching zebra finch (blue; 𝑛 = 238 units) reveal single-unit signatures consistent with the population-level crystalline code identified by Shesha analysis (Figure 1). (A) Spatial information (bits/spike) is substantially elevated in chickadees, consistent with higher per-neuron coding fidelity in the high-capacity regime. (B) Firing regularity (𝐶𝑉2) is modestly but significantly elevated in chickadees (𝑝 = 0.007), reflecting increased temporal precision of place-selective spiking. (C) Spatial coverage is near-ceiling for both species, reflecting the open-field recording design rather than species-specific biological difference. (D) Within-session map stability, quantified via split-half spatial map correlation, is markedly higher in chickadees (𝑝 = 7.0 1015)a single-unit analogue of the Shesha effect, confirming that geometric stability is manifest at the level of individual place fields. (E) Mean firing rate distributions; chickadees exhibit higher rates on average, consistent with excitatory-dominant drive identified in the circuit analysis (Figure 2). (F) Fraction of spatially selective units by species and putative cell type. The excitatoryinhibitory selectivity asymmetry is larger in chickadees (E: 62%, I: 68%) than in zebra finches (E: 36%, I: 28%), consistent with excitatory neurons serving as the primary structural nodes of the crystalline code. For violin plots (AE), internal dots represent the median, thick vertical lines span the interquartile range, and shaded regions denote kernel density estimates. All comparisons are two-sided Mann-Whitney 𝑈 tests.
